name: Flathead Lake
description:
Flathead Lake is the largest natural freshwater lake in the western United States, a stunningly clear body of water nestled in northwestern Montana's Mission Valley. The lake's remarkable water clarity allows visibility to depths exceeding 30 feet.

Lake trout are the premier species, with fish commonly exceeding 20 pounds and trophy specimens reaching 40 pounds. The lake's lake trout suppression program encourages harvest to protect native bull trout and cutthroat trout. Yellow perch provide excellent panfishing. Multiple public launches around the 28-mile-long lake provide access from different vantage points.

name: Fort Peck Lake
description:
Fort Peck Lake is Montana's largest reservoir and one of the five largest in the United States, stretching 134 miles along the Missouri River in eastern Montana's prairies. The lake's enormous size and remote location create a true wilderness fishing experience.

Walleye are the primary target, with the lake producing fish over 10 pounds. Northern pike grow to trophy sizes in the lake's many bays and inlets. Chinook salmon, stocked by Montana FWP, provide exciting open-water trolling. The lake's immense shoreline means uncrowded fishing even during peak seasons. Several developed launches serve different areas.

name: Canyon Ferry Lake
description:
Canyon Ferry Lake is one of the most popular fishing destinations in southwestern Montana, located just 20 miles east of Helena on the Missouri River. The reservoir provides outstanding fishing for both trout and walleye.

Rainbow trout are the premier species, with fish commonly reaching 18-22 inches. Walleye fishing has become increasingly popular, with the lake producing consistent catches. Perch fishing through the ice during winter draws large crowds. The Missouri River below the dam is a legendary trout stream. Multiple Bureau of Reclamation ramps provide access around the lake.

name: Georgetown Lake
description:
Georgetown Lake is a high-altitude reservoir at 6,330 feet in the Flint Creek Range of southwestern Montana. The lake is renowned for producing large rainbow trout and supporting a rare population of Arctic grayling.

Rainbow trout exceeding 20 inches are common, with the lake's prolific insect hatches providing outstanding dry-fly fishing. Kokanee salmon offer exciting fishing during their fall spawning run. The Arctic grayling population makes Georgetown one of the few places in the lower 48 where this species can be caught. A boat launch on the west shore provides the primary access.

name: Missouri River (MT)
description:
The Missouri River in Montana is one of the premier trout fisheries in the world, particularly the tailwater section below Holter Dam near Craig. This legendary reach produces rainbow and brown trout in exceptional numbers and sizes.

The Craig section averages over 5,000 trout per mile, with fish commonly exceeding 20 inches. The river's prolific insect hatches — particularly the Mother's Day caddis and summer trichoptera — create world-class dry-fly fishing. Below Great Falls, the river transitions to an excellent warm-water fishery for walleye and smallmouth bass. Multiple fishing access sites provide drift boat and wade fishing access.

name: Yellowstone River
description:
The Yellowstone River is the longest undammed river in the lower 48 states, flowing 692 miles from Yellowstone National Park to the Missouri River. The upper river near Livingston is one of Montana's finest trout streams.

Native Yellowstone cutthroat trout are the signature species in the upper reaches, with brown and rainbow trout increasingly prevalent downstream. The spring salmonfly hatch on the Yellowstone is one of fly fishing's iconic events, producing explosive surface strikes. Multiple fishing access sites along the river provide wade and float fishing opportunities throughout its course.

name: Holter Lake
description:
Holter Lake is a narrow reservoir on the Missouri River upstream of the famous Craig trout section, situated in a dramatic limestone canyon near Wolf Creek. The lake provides both excellent fishing and stunning scenery.

Rainbow trout are abundant and grow quickly in the productive water, with fish regularly reaching 16-20 inches. Walleye provide consistent catches, especially during spring and fall. The Gates of the Mountains scenic area within the lake offers breathtaking canyon walls rising hundreds of feet. Multiple launches including the Log Gulch and Departure Point ramps provide access.
